narrative_blocks.header: His church split; he founded another
narrative_blocks.body: The half-way covenant fight broke Hartford’s First Church in 1669, and thirty-two members walked out. Whiting petitioned the Assembly “for a distinct walking in Congregational Church Order,” won, and was ordained pastor of the new Second Church — which celebrated its 350th anniversary in 2020.

narrative_blocks.header: His bride’s father sailed the phantom ship
narrative_blocks.body: His second wife Phebe was the daughter of Thomas Gregson of New Haven, lost at sea in 1646 aboard the famous “phantom ship.” Phebe outlived Whiting by forty-one years, remarrying Rev. John Russell of Hadley.

narrative_blocks.header: His church split; he founded another
narrative_blocks.body: The half-way covenant fight broke Hartford’s First Church in 1669, and thirty-two members walked out. Whiting petitioned the Assembly “for a distinct walking in Congregational Church Order,” won, and was ordained pastor of the new Second Church — which celebrated its 350th anniversary in 2020.

narrative_blocks.header: His bride’s father sailed the phantom ship
narrative_blocks.body: His second wife Phebe was the daughter of Thomas Gregson of New Haven, lost at sea in 1646 aboard the famous “phantom ship.” Phebe outlived Whiting by forty-one years, remarrying Rev. John Russell of Hadley.

bio.bio_blurb: First wife of Rev. John Whiting

narrative_blocks.header: A minister's first wife, gone too soon
narrative_blocks.body: Sibyl Collins married Rev. John Whiting around 1654 and bore his earliest children before her death in 1672. Daughter of Edward Collins and sister of Rev. John Collins, she connected two ministerial families of early New England.

narrative_blocks.header: Her sons commanded armies and pulpits
narrative_blocks.body: Two of Sibyl's sons rose to prominence: Col. William Whiting led Connecticut troops against French and Indian forces and later served as High Sheriff of Hartford County, while Rev. Samuel Whiting became the first minister of Windham, preaching his first sermon there on New Year's Day 1693.

bio.bio_blurb: Wife of a minister; mother of Whiting leaders

narrative_blocks.header: Twice wed, to a merchant then a magistrate
narrative_blocks.body: Martha Whiting married Samuel Bryan of Milford in 1683 and, after his early death, the colonel and judge Samuel Eells in 1700. Daughter of Rev. John Whiting of Hartford, she bridged a ministerial family and Milford's merchant class.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father preached; her brothers led
narrative_blocks.body: Martha was born to Rev. John Whiting's first marriage, to Sibyl Collins. Her brothers Col. William Whiting and Rev. Samuel Whiting rose to military and ministerial prominence across early Connecticut.

bio.bio_blurb: Milford merchant of the colonial Bryan family

narrative_blocks.header: A Milford merchant gone before forty
narrative_blocks.body: Samuel Bryan of Milford married Martha Whiting in 1683 and fathered the Bryan daughters of that town, including Susannah, who married Col. Roger Newton. He died around 1698, still a young man, leaving Martha to remarry the magistrate Samuel Eells.

narrative_blocks.header: His daughter married into the Hooker line
narrative_blocks.body: Through his daughter Susannah's marriage to Col. Roger Newton, Samuel Bryan became grandfather to a branch of Thomas Hooker's descendants. His own father, Richard Bryan, tied the family to the prominent Bryan merchants of the New Haven Colony.

bio.title: Colonel
bio.bio_blurb: Colonel and judge of colonial Connecticut

narrative_blocks.header: A private pew, then a colonel’s command
narrative_blocks.body: Granted liberty in 1711 to build a private pew for his family, Samuel Eells rose to Colonel of the New Haven County regiment in 1739, holding that command until 1752. He married the widowed Martha Whiting Bryan in 1700.

narrative_blocks.header: He judged from four different benches
narrative_blocks.body: Eells served the colony as Deputy to the General Court across twelve sessions and sat as judge on the Superior Court of Judicature, the New Haven County Court, and the Probate court, while also acting as Naval Officer for the port of Milford.

bio.discrepancy_note: Edward Hooker gives primary surname as Bryan; a second account gives Baldwin. Both accounts agree on father Samuel and mother Martha (Whiting). Bryan preferred as primary account.

sources.full: Phebe (Grigson) Russell: third wife of Rev. John Russell, widow of Rev. John Whiting; d. 19 Sep 1730, New Haven.

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of the phantom ship's lost merchant
narrative_blocks.body: Phebe's father, Thomas Gregson of New Haven, sailed in the colony's famous “phantom ship” that vanished at sea around 1647. The ghostly vessel later said to appear in the harbor became New England legend. Phebe carried that haunted lineage into two marriages.

narrative_blocks.header: She outlived two Hartford-area ministers
narrative_blocks.body: Widow of Rev. John Whiting of Hartford, Phebe married Rev. John Russell of Hadley in 1692. She survived him as well, then removed to New Haven. She died there in 1730, near ninety.

narrative_blocks.header: Grandson of a Hartford founder
narrative_blocks.body: His grandfather Major William Whiting was one of Hartford's founding settlers. His father Rev. John Whiting was the 4th Minister of Hartford. He became Hon. Col. Joseph Whiting of New Haven — and his children's double marriage to the Ingersolls would eventually connect this Hartford founding family line to Jared Ingersoll Jr., signer of the U.S. Constitution.

cross_connections.display_label: Their children made a double sibling marriage — son×daughter, daughter×son.
Their children made a double sibling marriage — son×daughter, daughter×son.

burial.plot_notes: Memorialized at bottom of shared stone with husband Jared in Center Church Crypt

narrative_blocks.header: The Ingersoll and Whiting families exchanged siblings
narrative_blocks.body: She married John Whiting; her brother Jared Ingersoll Sr. married John's sister Hannah Whiting. Two Ingersoll siblings and two Whiting siblings, cross-married in the same generation — the families of New Haven thoroughly interlaced.

narrative_blocks.header: The Ingersoll and Whiting families exchanged siblings
narrative_blocks.body: She married John Whiting; her brother Jared Ingersoll Sr. married John's sister Hannah Whiting. Two Ingersoll siblings and two Whiting siblings, cross-married in the same generation — the families of New Haven thoroughly interlaced.

narrative_blocks.header: Son of Hon. Col. Joseph Whiting
narrative_blocks.body: His father Hon. Col. Joseph Whiting was a grandson of Major William Whiting, one of Hartford's founders, and son of Rev. John Whiting, the 4th Minister of Hartford. His sister Hannah Whiting married Jared Ingersoll Sr., the Connecticut Stamp Master whose son Jared Jr. signed the Constitution.

political_offices.title: Treasurer of the Colony of Connecticut

cross_connections.notes: Both appear in Hartford records from 1639. Whiting died in 1647, the same year as Thomas Hooker.
cross_connections.display_label: led the company Whiting joined to found Hartford

sources.short: Founders of Hartford — Major William Whiting

narrative_blocks.header: Hartford founder — in Hartford by 1639
narrative_blocks.body: Hartford founder — in Hartford by 1639; held extensive land in South Meadow, Oxpasture, and Hockanum

narrative_blocks.header: Colony Treasurer of Connecticut 1641–1647
narrative_blocks.body: Colony Treasurer of Connecticut 1641–1647

narrative_blocks.header: Colony Magistrate 1641–1647
narrative_blocks.body: Colony Magistrate 1641–1647

narrative_blocks.header: In 1646, target of assassination plot by Sequasson
narrative_blocks.body: In 1646, target of assassination plot by Sequasson, Sachem of the Naticks — plot to kill Governors Haynes and Hopkins and Whiting, disclosed by a friendly Indian

narrative_blocks.header: Styled in colonial records as "William Whiting, Gentleman" —…
narrative_blocks.body: Styled in colonial records as "William Whiting, Gentleman" — "one of the most respectable of the settlers of 1635"

narrative_blocks.header: His trade ran to four colonies
narrative_blocks.body: “William Whiting, Gentleman” traded in Delaware, Long Island, Virginia, Piscataqua, and old England, and held the Piscataqua patent with Lords Say and Brooke and George Wyllys. The records call him one of the fathers of the colony.

narrative_blocks.header: He amended his will on his deathbed
narrative_blocks.body: His first will of 1643 was made “in consideration that he intended a voyage on the sea”; the last addition came July 24, 1647 — the day he died. He had been the colony’s treasurer to the end.

bio.notes: Maiden name uncertain — given as Wiggins in some sources

burialCemetery.name: Ancient Burying Ground
burialCemetery.notes: Adjacent to Center Church. Earliest surviving identifiable gravestone dates to 1648. Resting place of many Hartford founders including Rev. Thomas Hooker (exact location unknown).

INST003.name: First Church of Christ, Hartford
INST003.notes: Traces roots to village of Little Baddow, Essex County, England. Moved to current spot adjacent Ancient Burying Ground in 1739.
